cards.front: What type of relationship does inheritance model in C++?
cards.back: An "is-a" relationship between two objects (e.g., an Apple is-a Fruit).

cards.text: In inheritance, the class being inherited from is called the {{c1::parent class}}, {{c1::base class}}, or {{c1::superclass}}, while the class doing the inheriting is called the {{c2::child class}}, {{c2::derived class}}, or {{c2::subclass}}.

cards.front: What is the difference between composition ("has-a") and inheritance ("is-a") relationships?
cards.back: Composition models a "has-a" relationship where an object contains other objects as parts. Inheritance models an "is-a" relationship where a derived object IS a type of the base object.

cards.text: To create a derived class that publicly inherits from a base class: {{c1::class Derived: public Base}}

cards.front: What is a hierarchy in the context of inheritance?
cards.back: A diagram that shows how various objects are related, either showing a progression over time or categorizing things from general to specific.

cards.explanation: Inheritance is everywhere in real life. Biological inheritance (children inherit genes from parents), technology (Intel Pentium inherited features from 486), and categories (Apple is-a Fruit, which is-a Food).
examples: Apple and Banana inherit properties from Fruit
Square is-a Rectangle is-a Quadrilateral is-a Shape
C++ inherited features from C language

cards.text: Inheritance enables {{c1::polymorphism}} through the use of {{c2::virtual functions}}.

cards.front: If apples and bananas are fruits, and all fruits have a name, color, and size, what can we conclude about apples and bananas?
cards.back: Apples and bananas also have a name, color, and size. They inherit (acquire) all properties of fruit because they ARE fruit.

cards.text: In an inheritance hierarchy, items at the {{c1::top}} are more {{c1::general}}, while items at the {{c2::bottom}} are more {{c2::specific}}.

cards.front: What are the two main benefits of using inheritance mentioned in the introduction?
cards.back:
1) Reusing already written code
2) Modeling is-a relationships between objects more naturally
